summary: In the 2025 Berkshire Hathaway annual meeting, Warren Buffett discusses his long-term investment philosophy, the rationale behind holding massive cash reserves, and the superiority of stocks over real estate. He also shares thoughts on global trade, the importance of acting decisively when rare opportunities arise, and life advice on finding work you love.

principles.statement: Capital allocation should be opportunistic, not scheduled.
principles.rationale: Investment opportunities do not appear in an orderly or predictable fashion. It is better to hold large amounts of cash and wait for extraordinary opportunities than to invest on a forced timeline.
principles.source_quote: If you told me that I had to invest $50 billion every year until we got down to $50 billion in cash, that would be the dumbest thing in the world.

principles.statement: Stocks are vastly superior to real estate as an investment vehicle.
principles.rationale: Stock transactions are fast, anonymous, and have a near 100% completion rate once a price is agreed upon. Real estate involves protracted negotiations, multiple parties, and immense time commitments.
principles.source_quote: The completion rate for working on anything in stocks, assuming you've got a meeting of the minds on price, is essentially 100%. In real estate, the negotiation just begins when you agree on deals, and then they take forever.

principles.statement: Never make business decisions based on their impact on quarterly or annual earnings.
principles.rationale: Focusing on short-term earnings targets tempts management to manipulate numbers, which compromises the long-term integrity and success of the business.
principles.source_quote: If you start focusing on what number you're going to produce, you will quickly get tempted to play around with the numbers and sometimes seriously play around with the numbers.

principles.statement: Trade should be balanced and never used as a weapon.
principles.rationale: Using trade as an act of economic war breeds global resentment and instability. Countries should focus on what they do best and trade mutually for shared prosperity.
principles.source_quote: The main thing is that trade should not be a weapon... it's a big mistake when you have 7.5 billion people who don't like you very well and you have 300 million people crowing about how well they've done.

frameworks.name: Turn Every Page
frameworks.when_to_apply: When searching for undervalued investment opportunities in large, overlooked markets.
steps: Obtain comprehensive directories or handbooks of businesses (e.g., a handbook of thousands of Japanese companies).
Methodically read through the data, page by page, looking for anomalies or ridiculously low prices.
Investigate the most promising leads deeply before initiating a position.
frameworks.source_quote: I would say that turning every page is one important ingredient to bring to the investment field. Very few people do turn every page, and the ones who turn every page aren't going to tell you what they're finding.

mental_models.name: The Treasure Hunt
mental_models.description: Viewing the investment research process not as a daily grind of predictable returns, but as a long, irregular search through massive amounts of information to occasionally uncover a rare, highly valuable prize.
mental_models.example: Leafing through a handbook of two or three thousand Japanese companies to find five trading companies selling at ridiculously low prices.

mental_models.name: The Uneven Flow
mental_models.description: The understanding that opportunities in business and markets do not arrive on a smooth, predictable schedule. You must accept long periods of inactivity punctuated by moments requiring massive, immediate action.
mental_models.example: Holding $335 billion in treasuries while waiting for the rare moment when the market bombards them with attractive offerings.

heuristics: Size is an enemy of performance.
You have to be willing to hang up after 5 seconds and you have to be willing to say yes after 5 seconds.
Don't worry too much about starting salaries and be very careful who you work for.
You don't want to be patient when the time comes to act – you want to get it done that day.

signature_quotes.text: We have made a lot of money by not wanting to be fully invested at all times.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why Berkshire Hathaway holds over $300 billion in cash and short-term investments.

signature_quotes.text: The main thing is you have to be willing to hang up after 5 seconds and you have to be willing to say yes after 5 seconds. You can't be filled with self-doubt in this business.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the balance between extreme patience and the need to act instantly when a good deal arrives.

signature_quotes.text: Size is an enemy of performance at Berkshire, and I don't know any good way to solve that problem.
signature_quotes.context: Lamenting that Berkshire cannot easily deploy $100 billion into great ideas like the Japanese trading companies because of the sheer scale required to move the needle.

signature_quotes.text: I wouldn't trade everything that's developed in AI in the next 10 years for Ajit.
signature_quotes.context: Praising Ajit Jain's value to Berkshire Hathaway's insurance operations over the potential of artificial intelligence.

signature_quotes.text: If you start focusing on what number you're going to produce, you will quickly get tempted to play around with the numbers and sometimes seriously play around with the numbers.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why Berkshire does not manage its business or currency hedging to smooth out quarterly earnings.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Markets do not offer attractive opportunities on a predictable schedule. Forcing capital deployment leads to buying mediocre assets at bad prices.

anti_patterns.description: Managing a business to hit quarterly or annual earnings targets.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It inevitably tempts management to manipulate accounting numbers rather than focusing on the long-term reality of the business.

anti_patterns.description: Participating in speculative frenzies or 'stupid things'.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Following crowds into junk securities or highly leveraged bets just because others are making money will eventually 'bite you' when the bubble bursts.

anti_patterns.description: Using trade as an act of economic war.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates global envy and instability, making the world more dangerous rather than fostering mutual prosperity.
